text.title: Service Mesh Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding Service Mesh. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale. The {skip-this-methodology} rule routes inapplicable cases.

rule.statement: Prefer sidecarless architectures (Istio Ambient or Cilium eBPF) for new deployments in 2025; use traditional Envoy sidecars only when full per-pod L7 isolation is a hard requirement.

rationale.source: Istio Ambient docs, 2025 mesh benchmarks; Solo.io / Tetrate performance reports

rule.rationale: Ambient mode reduces latency overhead from +166% to +8% and eliminates per-pod sidecar memory cost. Most production use cases (mTLS, canary routing, basic observability) get equivalent functionality with significantly lower resource cost.

rule.statement: Start mTLS in permissive mode to establish baseline metrics, then migrate to strict mode namespace-by-namespace after validating every service-to-service path is enrolled with certificates.

rationale.source: Istio security docs; Linkerd production patterns

rule.rationale: Strict mode without permissive validation silently breaks external services, legacy apps, and any service with annotation exceptions. Permissive mode allows both plaintext and mTLS, giving operators a safe window to verify coverage before enforcement.

rule.statement: Use organisation-signed intermediate CA (cert-manager + Vault PKI or organisation PKI); certificate TTL ≤ 24h with automatic rotation; never use self-signed roots in production.
rule.rationale: Long-lived certs widen credential-theft windows; self-signed roots break revocation and audit trail. Mesh control planes rotate automatically; the only operator job is to configure the rotation period and monitor certificate expiry as a service-level metric.

rule.statement: Configure outlier detection on every service VirtualDestination (or equivalent): eject endpoints returning consecutive 5xx errors for a minimum ejection period of 30s before re-admission.
rule.rationale: A degraded upstream pod returning 5xx to every request continues to receive its weighted traffic share without outlier detection. Passive health monitoring at the proxy removes unhealthy endpoints without explicit health-check infrastructure.

rule.statement: Canary deployments progress through bounded weight steps (1% → 10% → 50% → 100%) gated on SLO metrics (error rate, p99 latency) measured per step; rollback by shifting weight back to the stable version.

rationale.source: Argo Rollouts docs; Flagger canary patterns

rule.rationale: Big-bang weight jumps blow past SLO breach detection. Bounded steps let the SLO observer halt progression before the blast radius reaches all traffic.

rule.statement: VirtualServices, DestinationRules, AuthorizationPolicies and Gateways are committed to the repo and applied via GitOps (Argo CD, Flux). Manual `kubectl apply` for mesh config is forbidden in production.

rationale.source: GitOps with Argo CD docs; Linkerd best practices

rule.rationale: Hand-applied mesh config drifts between clusters and is invisible to PR review. GitOps gives history, review, and rollback for every traffic-routing change.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the team has <5 services, the workload is a monolith, or sub-1ms latency is mandatory.

rationale.source: Internal cost/benefit analyses; mesh adoption surveys

rule.rationale: Mesh proxies add a measurable latency floor and operational complexity. Below 5 services, direct mTLS via cert-manager is simpler; for sub-1ms latency, any proxy layer is too expensive.
